1: Livestock production is indicated to be one of the major emitters of greenhouse gases (GHG), particularly methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20), around the globe, and the reduction of these emissions is an important goal. GHG emissions as well as other environmental impacts of two pig (Sus scrofa domesticus) farming systems, one using conventional diets (CNV) and the other using low-protein diets supplemented with crystalline amino acids (LOW), were therefore evaluated by comparative life cycle assessment (LCA) focusing on manure management and by cradle-to-farm gate LCA. The functional unit was defined as one marketed pig. For the comparative LCA of manure management, the CH4 and N2O emissions from manure management of CNV were set as a baseline, and the system boundary of LOW included the CH4 and N2O emissions from manure management, and changes in the GHG emissions from feed production including amino acid manufacturing, feed transport, and the materials and energy consumed in manure management. For the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming, the evaluated system included the processes of feed production including amino acid manufacturing for LOW, feed transport, animal housing including the biological activity of the animal, and manure management. The results of the comparative LCA showed that the GHG emissions from manure management of LOW were 20% less than those of CNV, and the GHG reduction rate of LOW compared to CNV was even greater in the case of a stricter target of effluent nitrogen content. The results of cradle-to-farm gate LCA showed that LOW had lower GHG emissions, acidification potential, eutrophication potential and overall environmental impact, and slightly larger energy consumption, than CNV. The sensitivity analysis showed that LOW still had less GHG emissions than CNV, even in the least preferable case assuming a 40% lower reduction rate of nitrogen excretion.
Keywords: swine, life cycle analysis, greenhouse gas, low-protein diet, amino acids.

greenhouse gases (GHG), particularly methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20), worldwide (Steinfeld et al. 2006). In Japan, livestock production accounts for one- fourth of the national agricultural output, while it was estimated to generate 88 million tonnes of manure annually in 2009 (MAFF 2012). The GHG emissions from livestock manure management account for 27% of the total emissions from the agricultural sector (MOE 2010), and the reduction of these emissions is an important goal. However, reducing emissions through the modification of manure treatment techniques is not
easy because manure management is implemented in response to manure characteristics and the purpose of manure utilization.

One possible measure to achieve GHG reduction is reducing the animal excretion of nitrogen (N), which is a source of N2O emissions in manure management. Many studies of the feeding of a low-protein diet supplemented with crystalline amino acids (AA) to fattening pigs (Sus scrofa domesticus) have been conducted and have reported a reduction of N excretion without sacrificing productivity (Han and Lee 2000; Otto et al. 2003b; Rotz 2004). It has also been reported that such a diet reduces ammonia (NH3) emissions from manure (Canh et al. 1998; Otto et al. 2003a) and, furthermore, the reduction of N2O emissions from manure has recently been reported to be achieved by the low-protein diet technique (Osada et al. 2011). The feeding of a low-protein diet with AA is therefore expected to be a measure that could achieve GHG reduction without depending on the modification of manure treatment techniques.

However, the use of a low-protein diet with AA involves additional GHG emissions resulting from AA manufacturing and affects the emissions from feed production, and those derived from the energy and materials consumed in manure management, due to changes in the compositions of the diet and manure. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a suitable method for environ- mental evaluation in such a case (ISO 2006). The LCA method has been applied to evaluate GHG emissions from pig farming (Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005; Liang et al. 2007; Nguyen et al. 2010) as well as other livestock production systems such as dairy farming (Ogino et al. 2008; Thomassen et al. 2008) and beef production (Ogino et al. 2004; Casey and Holden 2006). However, the GHG emissions from whole life cycle (cradle-to-farm gate life cycle) of pig farming using a low-protein diet with AA have not yet been evaluated. Before extensive promotion of the low-protein diet technique takes place for the purpose of GHG reduction, the technique needs to be evaluated in terms of GHG emissions throughout its life cycle and in terms of the trade-offs against other environmental impacts such as acidification and eutrophication.

The objectives of the present study are: (i) to conduct comparative LCA of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in two pig farming systems, one using conventional diets and the other using the low- protein diets with AA; (ii) to evaluate the environmental impacts of the cradle-to-farm gate life cycle of the two pig farming systems; and (iii) to analyze the sensitivity of GHG emissions resulting from manure management to conditions and key parameters in order to verify the robustness of the results.

7: The first step of LCA is the definition of the goal and scope of the analysis, the functional unit, and the system boundaries. The functional unit is a reference to which all other materials (and also the associated environmen- tal loads) in the LCA are related. Examples of impact categories are global warming, acidification, and eutro- phication. The functional unit was defined as one marketed pig. In the present study, the goal of the analysis was to evaluate and compare the environmental impacts of two types of Japanese pig farming systems, one of which makes use of low-protein diets supple- mented with AA (LOW) and the other of which uses conventional diets (CNV). An outline of the systems analyzed in this study is presented in Fig. 1. For the comparative LCA of manure management, the CH4 and N2O emissions from the manure management of CNV were set as a baseline, and the system boundary of LOW included the CH4 and N2O emissions resulting from manure management and from changes in the GHG emissions from the processes of feed production, includ- ing AA manufacturing and feed transport and the materials and energy consumed in the manure manage- ment process. For the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming, the evaluated system included the processes of feed production, including AA manufacturing for LOW, feed transport, animal housing including the biological activity of the animal, and manure management. With the low-protein diet technique, the N content in waste- water is reduced, and then the N discharge after wastewater treatment is also reduced. Aeration for nitrification followed by denitrification, however, could be softened to reduce GHG emissions within the effluent standard for N instead of reducing the N discharge. Thus, environmental impacts were also investigated with the goal of attaining the maximum reduction of GHG emissions. A tentative effluent standard for nitrate- nitrogen, 900 mg L-1, is now applied to pig farms in Japan, and it could be dropped to the general standard, 100 mg L-1, in the future. Thus, the comparative LCA of manure management and the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming were also conducted with a target N content level in effluent from wastewater treatment of 100 mg L-¹.

The basic pig production cycle was defined as follows: a SOW was first inseminated at 8 months of age, she produced 10 pigs every 153 d (a gestation and non- productive period of 128 d and a lactation period of 25d), and the SOW was then culled at 38 months of age after six parturitions (60 pigs were produced by a SOW as a whole). The fattening pigs produced were defined to be

It was assumed that all produced fattening pigs were shipped out, and the culled SOW was also shipped. These previous studies focused on feeding a low-protein diet with AA and a conventional diet to growing pigs and fattening pigs, respectively, and investigated N excretion and productivity. The weight gain of pigs during the growing and fattening stages was defined as the same between LOW and CNV based on the results of these studies. nutrient requirements for swine (NARO 2005, 2010) and were identical between LOW and CNV. A growth curve for the pigs was obtained based on the daily gain in the literature (NARO 2005), and it was used to calculate the nutrient requirements and amounts of N and carbon (C) excreted into manure. In the present pig farming systems, the feed ingredients were purchased, and the pigs were fed these feeds in the barn. Pig solid manure (80% of the total feces excreted) was assumed to be treated by composting with forced aeration and subsequent biofiltration for odor control, and liquid manure (20% of the feces and all the urine excreted) was assumed to be treated by activated-sludge wastewater treatment, as these are the typical treatments in Japan (MOE 2010). The pig farming system produces not only pigs but also finished compost as a by-product that is used on other farmlands as organic fertilizer, and thus economic allocation was conducted. The proportion of the environmental load of a pig was calculated to be 99.4% of the total on the basis of the sale prices of the pigs and compost (MAFF 2010b). The environmental loads associated with the production of capital goods such as pig barns and composting facilities were also not taken into account.

9: The first step of LCA is the definition of the goal and scope of the analysis, the functional unit, and the system boundaries. The functional unit is a reference to which all other materials (and also the associated environmen- tal loads) in the LCA are related. Examples of impact categories are global warming, acidification, and eutro- phication. The functional unit was defined as one marketed pig. In the present study, the goal of the analysis was to evaluate and compare the environmental impacts of two types of Japanese pig farming systems, one of which makes use of low-protein diets supple- mented with AA (LOW) and the other of which uses conventional diets (CNV). An outline of the systems analyzed in this study is presented in Fig. 1. For the comparative LCA of manure management, the CH4 and N2O emissions from the manure management of CNV were set as a baseline, and the system boundary of LOW included the CH4 and N2O emissions resulting from manure management and from changes in the GHG emissions from the processes of feed production, includ- ing AA manufacturing and feed transport and the materials and energy consumed in the manure manage- ment process. For the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming, the evaluated system included the processes of feed production, including AA manufacturing for LOW, feed transport, animal housing including the biological activity of the animal, and manure management. With the low-protein diet technique, the N content in waste- water is reduced, and then the N discharge after wastewater treatment is also reduced. Aeration for nitrification followed by denitrification, however, could be softened to reduce GHG emissions within the effluent standard for N instead of reducing the N discharge. Thus, environmental impacts were also investigated with the goal of attaining the maximum reduction of GHG emissions. A tentative effluent standard for nitrate- nitrogen, 900 mg L-1, is now applied to pig farms in Japan, and it could be dropped to the general standard, 100 mg L-1, in the future. Thus, the comparative LCA of manure management and the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming were also conducted with a target N content level in effluent from wastewater treatment of 100 mg L-¹.

The basic pig production cycle was defined as follows: a SOW was first inseminated at 8 months of age, she produced 10 pigs every 153 d (a gestation and non- productive period of 128 d and a lactation period of 25d), and the SOW was then culled at 38 months of age after six parturitions (60 pigs were produced by a SOW as a whole). The fattening pigs produced were defined to be

13: The first step of LCA is the definition of the goal and scope of the analysis, the functional unit, and the system boundaries. The functional unit is a reference to which all other materials (and also the associated environmen- tal loads) in the LCA are related. Examples of impact categories are global warming, acidification, and eutro- phication. The functional unit was defined as one marketed pig. In the present study, the goal of the analysis was to evaluate and compare the environmental impacts of two types of Japanese pig farming systems, one of which makes use of low-protein diets supple- mented with AA (LOW) and the other of which uses conventional diets (CNV). An outline of the systems analyzed in this study is presented in Fig. 1. For the comparative LCA of manure management, the CH4 and N2O emissions from the manure management of CNV were set as a baseline, and the system boundary of LOW included the CH4 and N2O emissions resulting from manure management and from changes in the GHG emissions from the processes of feed production, includ- ing AA manufacturing and feed transport and the materials and energy consumed in the manure manage- ment process. For the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming, the evaluated system included the processes of feed production, including AA manufacturing for LOW, feed transport, animal housing including the biological activity of the animal, and manure management. With the low-protein diet technique, the N content in waste- water is reduced, and then the N discharge after wastewater treatment is also reduced. Aeration for nitrification followed by denitrification, however, could be softened to reduce GHG emissions within the effluent standard for N instead of reducing the N discharge. Thus, environmental impacts were also investigated with the goal of attaining the maximum reduction of GHG emissions. A tentative effluent standard for nitrate- nitrogen, 900 mg L-1, is now applied to pig farms in Japan, and it could be dropped to the general standard, 100 mg L-1, in the future. Thus, the comparative LCA of manure management and the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming were also conducted with a target N content level in effluent from wastewater treatment of 100 mg L-¹.

15: The second step of LCA is to draw up an inventory of all the resources used and all the emissions released into the environment connected with all processes within the system boundary. The emission factors associated with pig farming considered in the analysis are shown in Table 2. The emission factors used in this study were adopted because they were determined to be the most applicable to the defined conditions of the processes in the systems analyzed, such as the climate, animals, and styles of housing and waste treatment. For example, the emission factor suggested by Harper et al. (2004) was used to calculate NH3 emission from the pig barn

considering the conditions of housing such as the slat floor and the daily removal of manure, rather than the storage of manure in an under-floor pit for a long period. To calculate the environmental loads from the produc- tion and combustion of fossil fuels, the consumption of electricity, the production of materials, and marine transport, the database of the LCA software JEMAI-LCA Pro (JEMAI 2005) was used, and if the data for materials were lacking in the database, input- output-based databases were used (NIES 2002; Ajinomoto 2007).

Because most of the feeds for Japanese pig production are imported mainly from the United States, the data on feed production in the United States were used. Pollutants emitted as a result of the production of commercial feed were determined using data on the amounts of agricultural materials for each crop (USDA- ERS 2009) and the fuels and electricity (Wang 2008) consumed in production. The data of Wang (2008) were also used for the unit emissions for each agricultural material. As for N2O emissions from the field of feed production, 1.325% of the N amount in the chemical and organic fertilizer applied to the fields and 1.225% of the N amount in the crop residues left on the fields were defined to be emitted as N2O directly and indirectly (IPCC 2006). As for NH3 emissions from the field of feed production, 6.8% of the N amount in chemical and organic fertilizer applied to the fields was defined to be emitted (Bouwman et al. 2002). With regard to AA used to supplement the low-protein diet in this study, L-lysine hydrochloric acid (HCI), L-threonine, and L-tryptophan are produced by fermentation, while DL-methionine is produced by chemical synthesis. The data regarding environmental emissions from the production of AA for Japanese pig producers were obtained from the manu- facturers. The weighted averages of GHG emissions from and energy consumption of the production of AA were 5.35 kg carbon dioxide equivalent (COe) kg-1 and 85.5 MJ respectively.

Environmental loads from the commercial feed trans- port to Japan were determined by multiplying the unit emission by the product of the feed weight and transport distance. The shares of the major feed-exporting countries to Japan, of which the sum of shares is more than 90% of the total, were obtained from published trade statistics (MOF 2008), and each share was divided by the sum of the major countries' shares SO that the sum of the calculated major countries' shares became 100%. The calculated shares of the exporting countries for each main feed crop were as follows: 100% from the United States for corn (Zea mays); and 81.4% from the United States, 11.0% from Brazil, and 7.5% from Canada for soybeans (Glycine max Merr.). The weighted averages of the environmental loads of feed transport from the major countries were calculated based on the calculated major countries' shares. Not only soymeal but also soy oil are produced from soybeans, and thus the environmental loads of soybean cultivation and its processing were partitioned between the two products based on the economic allocation method (Sato 2006). The unit emissions and energy consumption for land transport, marine transport, and rail and barge transport were based on Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry and Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (METI and MLIT 2007), Japan Environmental Management Association for Industry (JEMAI 2005), and Wang (2008), respectively.

Lighting of the pig barn, feed preparation, and carrying manure out of the barn were taken into account as work associated with animal management, and the environmental loads were calculated based on the quantities of fuel and electricity consumed in the work (AFFTIS 2000). For manure management, the environ- mental loads were calculated based on the quantities of fuel, electricity, and materials consumed during com- posting and wastewater treatment obtained using the model of the environmental evaluation program for pig manure management (CMETEL 2005). The amounts of N and phosphorus (P) discharge after wastewater treat- ment were also obtained using the model. The environ- mental loads emitted in association with the degradation of manure were determined using the emission factors shown in Table 2. It was defined that there was no NH3 emission from the composting process due to biofiltra- tion (Yasuda et al. 2009).

Enteric CH4 emissions by swine in the growing- fattening stage were calculated using the emission factor shown in Table 2, and for the stages of piglet and sow, the emission factor was adjusted in response to their feed intake compared to growing-fattening pigs. The amounts of feces were determined based on the digestibility of the ingredients in the diets. The N content
in excreted feces and urine for swine (expressed in g day-1) was calculated as follows: Excreted N = (CP - RP - MP)/6.25 (1)

where CP is crude protein intake day-1), RP is retained protein (g day-1), and MP is milk protein for the lactating SOW g day-1 calculated using the feeding standard (NARO 2005). The calculated N flow per functional unit from feed to manure in LOW and CNV was as follows, respectively: 9.05 and from feed intake, 3.04 and 3.04 used for weight gain, 1.67 and 1.59kgN excreted into feces, and 4.35 and 3.11 kgN excreted into urine.

The reduction of N excretion by the pigs resulting from the low-protein diets was determined to be 4.9% for feces and 40.6% for urine in the growing stage (Osada et al. 2011), and 7.0% for feces and 35.0% for urine in the fattening stage (Kaji et al. 1997). The organic matter content in excreted feces was calculated from the composition and the digestibility of each nutrient in the diets using the standard tables of feed composition (NARO 2010), and the typical value was used for the organic matter content in excreted urine (MOE 2010). The amounts and compositions of the diets were set to meet the nutrient requirements of swine in response to the growth stages. Also, the amount of manure from the diets was calculated, and the environmental loads related to manure were calculated by multiplying the N or organic matter in the manure by the coefficients. The production and environmental loads were therefore related to each other.

Based on these data, energy, carbon dioxide (CO2), CH4, N2O, NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx), and sulfur dioxide (SO2) were calculated either as resources or as emissions. Emissions of CO2 from swine respiration and the degradation of swine manure were offset by carbon fixation into forage crops from the atmosphere into forage crops through photosynthesis.

The weighted averages of GHG emissions from and energy consumption of the production of AA were 5.35 kg carbon dioxide equivalent (COe) kg-1 and 85.5 MJ respectively.

Environmental loads from the commercial feed trans- port to Japan were determined by multiplying the unit emission by the product of the feed weight and transport distance. The shares of the major feed-exporting countries to Japan, of which the sum of shares is more than 90% of the total, were obtained from published trade statistics (MOF 2008), and each share was divided by the sum of the major countries' shares SO that the sum of the calculated major countries' shares became 100%. The calculated shares of the exporting countries for each main feed crop were as follows: 100% from the United States for corn (Zea mays); and 81.4% from the United States, 11.0% from Brazil, and 7.5% from Canada for soybeans (Glycine max Merr.). The weighted averages of the environmental loads of feed transport from the major countries were calculated based on the calculated major countries' shares. Not only soymeal but also soy oil are produced from soybeans, and thus the environmental loads of soybean cultivation and its processing were partitioned between the two products based on the economic allocation method (Sato 2006). The unit emissions and energy consumption for land transport, marine transport, and rail and barge transport were based on Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry and Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (METI and MLIT 2007), Japan Environmental Management Association for Industry (JEMAI 2005), and Wang (2008), respectively.

Lighting of the pig barn, feed preparation, and carrying manure out of the barn were taken into account as work associated with animal management, and the environmental loads were calculated based on the quantities of fuel and electricity consumed in the work (AFFTIS 2000). For manure management, the environ- mental loads were calculated based on the quantities of fuel, electricity, and materials consumed during com- posting and wastewater treatment obtained using the model of the environmental evaluation program for pig manure management (CMETEL 2005). The amounts of N and phosphorus (P) discharge after wastewater treat- ment were also obtained using the model. The environ- mental loads emitted in association with the degradation of manure were determined using the emission factors shown in Table 2. It was defined that there was no NH3 emission from the composting process due to biofiltra- tion (Yasuda et al. 2009).

Enteric CH4 emissions by swine in the growing- fattening stage were calculated using the emission factor shown in Table 2, and for the stages of piglet and sow, the emission factor was adjusted in response to their feed intake compared to growing-fattening pigs. The amounts of feces were determined based on the digestibility of the ingredients in the diets. The N content
in excreted feces and urine for swine (expressed in g day-1) was calculated as follows: Excreted N = (CP - RP - MP)/6.25 (1)

where CP is crude protein intake day-1), RP is retained protein (g day-1), and MP is milk protein for the lactating SOW g day-1 calculated using the feeding standard (NARO 2005). The calculated N flow per functional unit from feed to manure in LOW and CNV was as follows, respectively: 9.05 and from feed intake, 3.04 and 3.04 used for weight gain, 1.67 and 1.59kgN excreted into feces, and 4.35 and 3.11 kgN excreted into urine.

The reduction of N excretion by the pigs resulting from the low-protein diets was determined to be 4.9% for feces and 40.6% for urine in the growing stage (Osada et al. 2011), and 7.0% for feces and 35.0% for urine in the fattening stage (Kaji et al. 1997). The organic matter content in excreted feces was calculated from the composition and the digestibility of each nutrient in the diets using the standard tables of feed composition (NARO 2010), and the typical value was used for the organic matter content in excreted urine (MOE 2010). The amounts and compositions of the diets were set to meet the nutrient requirements of swine in response to the growth stages. Also, the amount of manure from the diets was calculated, and the environmental loads related to manure were calculated by multiplying the N or organic matter in the manure by the coefficients. The production and environmental loads were therefore related to each other.

Based on these data, energy, carbon dioxide (CO2), CH4, N2O, NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx), and sulfur dioxide (SO2) were calculated either as resources or as emissions. Emissions of CO2 from swine respiration and the degradation of swine manure were offset by carbon fixation into forage crops from the atmosphere into forage crops through photosynthesis.

As for N2O emissions from the field of feed production, 1.325% of the N amount in the chemical and organic fertilizer applied to the fields and 1.225% of the N amount in the crop residues left on the fields were defined to be emitted as N2O directly and indirectly (IPCC 2006). As for NH3 emissions from the field of feed production, 6.8% of the N amount in chemical and organic fertilizer applied to the fields was defined to be emitted (Bouwman et al. 2002). With regard to AA used to supplement the low-protein diet in this study, L-lysine hydrochloric acid (HCI), L-threonine, and L-tryptophan are produced by fermentation, while DL-methionine is produced by chemical synthesis. The data regarding environmental emissions from the production of AA for Japanese pig producers were obtained from the manu- facturers. The weighted averages of GHG emissions from and energy consumption of the production of AA were 5.35 kg carbon dioxide equivalent (COe) kg-1 and 85.5 MJ respectively.

Environmental loads from the commercial feed trans- port to Japan were determined by multiplying the unit emission by the product of the feed weight and transport distance. The shares of the major feed-exporting countries to Japan, of which the sum of shares is more than 90% of the total, were obtained from published trade statistics (MOF 2008), and each share was divided by the sum of the major countries' shares SO that the sum of the calculated major countries' shares became 100%. The calculated shares of the exporting countries for each main feed crop were as follows: 100% from the United States for corn (Zea mays); and 81.4% from the United States, 11.0% from Brazil, and 7.5% from Canada for soybeans (Glycine max Merr.). The weighted averages of the environmental loads of feed transport from the major countries were calculated based on the calculated major countries' shares. Not only soymeal but also soy oil are produced from soybeans, and thus the environmental loads of soybean cultivation and its processing were partitioned between the two products based on the economic allocation method (Sato 2006). The unit emissions and energy consumption for land transport, marine transport, and rail and barge transport were based on Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry and Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (METI and MLIT 2007), Japan Environmental Management Association for Industry (JEMAI 2005), and Wang (2008), respectively.

Lighting of the pig barn, feed preparation, and carrying manure out of the barn were taken into account as work associated with animal management, and the environmental loads were calculated based on the quantities of fuel and electricity consumed in the work (AFFTIS 2000). For manure management, the environ- mental loads were calculated based on the quantities of fuel, electricity, and materials consumed during com- posting and wastewater treatment obtained using the model of the environmental evaluation program for pig manure management (CMETEL 2005). The amounts of N and phosphorus (P) discharge after wastewater treat- ment were also obtained using the model. The environ- mental loads emitted in association with the degradation of manure were determined using the emission factors shown in Table 2. It was defined that there was no NH3 emission from the composting process due to biofiltra- tion (Yasuda et al. 2009).

Enteric CH4 emissions by swine in the growing- fattening stage were calculated using the emission factor shown in Table 2, and for the stages of piglet and sow, the emission factor was adjusted in response to their feed intake compared to growing-fattening pigs. The amounts of feces were determined based on the digestibility of the ingredients in the diets. The N content
in excreted feces and urine for swine (expressed in g day-1) was calculated as follows: Excreted N = (CP - RP - MP)/6.25 (1)

where CP is crude protein intake day-1), RP is retained protein (g day-1), and MP is milk protein for the lactating SOW g day-1 calculated using the feeding standard (NARO 2005). The calculated N flow per functional unit from feed to manure in LOW and CNV was as follows, respectively: 9.05 and from feed intake, 3.04 and 3.04 used for weight gain, 1.67 and 1.59kgN excreted into feces, and 4.35 and 3.11 kgN excreted into urine.

The reduction of N excretion by the pigs resulting from the low-protein diets was determined to be 4.9% for feces and 40.6% for urine in the growing stage (Osada et al. 2011), and 7.0% for feces and 35.0% for urine in the fattening stage (Kaji et al. 1997). The organic matter content in excreted feces was calculated from the composition and the digestibility of each nutrient in the diets using the standard tables of feed composition (NARO 2010), and the typical value was used for the organic matter content in excreted urine (MOE 2010). The amounts and compositions of the diets were set to meet the nutrient requirements of swine in response to the growth stages. Also, the amount of manure from the diets was calculated, and the environmental loads related to manure were calculated by multiplying the N or organic matter in the manure by the coefficients. The production and environmental loads were therefore related to each other.

Based on these data, energy, carbon dioxide (CO2), CH4, N2O, NH3, nitrogen oxides (NOx), and sulfur dioxide (SO2) were calculated either as resources or as emissions. Emissions of CO2 from swine respiration and the degradation of swine manure were offset by carbon fixation into forage crops from the atmosphere into forage crops through photosynthesis.
23: In the present study, the contributions of the two pig farming systems were examined in relation to the environmental impact categories of global warming, acidification, eutrophication, and primary energy con- sumption. First, the data of the life cycle inventory were interpreted in terms of their environmental impact. The environmental loads were sorted and assigned to specific environmental impact categories, then multiplied by equivalency factors for each specific load and impact category. Thereafter, all of the weighted environmental loads included in the impact category were added, and the environmental impact was obtained. The global

warming potential (GWP), an index for estimating the global warming contribution due to the atmospheric emission of GHGs, was computed according to the CO2e factors defined by IPCC (IPCC 2007): CO2, 1; CH4, 25; and N2O, 298. These factors were set based on a time horizon of 100 years. To calculate the acidification potential of the different trace gases, the SO2 equivalent (SOe) factors for SO2 = 1, NOx = 0.7 and NH3 = 1.88 derived from Heijungs et al. (1992) were used. To calculate the eutrophication potential, the phosphate equivalent (PO4e) factors derived from Heijungs et al. (1992) for NOx=0.13, NH3 = 0.33, total nitrogen (TN) = 0.42, and total phosphorus (TP) = 3.06 were used.

As an option for further interpretation of the LCA results, weighting and integration of the environmental impact categories were conducted using the second version of the life-cycle impact assessment method based on endpoint modeling (LIME2). LIME2 is a method that has been developed taking the Japanese situation into account, as well as the occurrence of environmental loading and the environmental conscious- ness of citizens; it enables the integration of environ- mental impacts through damage assessment for some safeguard subjects such as human health and ecosystem, and weighting based on the results of conjoint analysis (Itsubo et al. 2004; Itsubo and Inaba 2010). Integrated environmental impacts were calculated according to the LIME2 integration factors expressed as yen kg- emis- sion for CO2=2.33, = CH4 = 61.9, and N2O = 738 in global warming; for SO2 = 120, NOx=95.7, = and NH3 = 736 in acidification; for NO3=18.6, NH4 = 64.1, TN = 82.5, and TP = 974 in eutrophication; and for crude oil - 2.83 in energy consumption (Itsubo and Inaba 2010). For energy consumption, the calculated total energy consumption expressed in megajoules was converted to kg of crude oil and was multiplied by the integration factor, because the integration factor is not classified into various petroleum products such as diesel and heavy oil.

The results of comparative LCA of GHG emissions resulting from manure management between the LOW and CNV diets are shown in Fig. 2. The GHG emissions from manure management in LOW and CNV were 80 and 100kg CO2e, respectively. The lower direct and indirect GHG emissions from manure management contributed to lower total GHG emissions from LOW in spite of the higher emissions from feed production including AA manufacturing. Figure 3 shows the results of comparative LCA of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in the LOW and CNV diets in the case in which the target N content in the effluent from wastewater treatment was 100 mg L-1. The GHG emis- sions for LOW were 24% smaller than those for CNV, and the GHG reduction rate was larger than in the case of the present effluent standard (Fig. 2) due to the large reduction in the indirect GHG emissions resulting from manure management.

Figure 4 shows the cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions from the pig farming systems. The GHG emissions from CNV were 370 kg COe in the present state, and LOW and LOW with maximum GHG reduction had the lower GHG emissions by 5.4% and 5.9%, respectively. diet supplemented with AA resulted in 20% lower GHG emissions, even taking into account negative effects of the changes in diet. While the GHG emissions from AA manufacturing were predictably higher than those from the production of the main feed ingredients such as corn and soybean meal, the reduction of N2O emissions from manure management, mainly from wastewater treat- ment, was much larger than the GHG increase due to the use of AA. In the case in which the target N content in

the effluent from wastewater treatment was 100 mg L-¹ the GHG reduction in manure management brought about by the low-protein diet technique was even larger (Fig. 3). This appeared to be because a larger amount of the C source for denitrification (methanol) was needed for CNV in this case to decrease the N content in the effluent SO as to meet the stricter effluent standard, while LOW had much lower urinary N excretion and lower subsequent N content in liquid manure, leading to the use of only a small amount of methanol. The national GHG inventory, which provides a basis for policy- making related to GHG mitigation, categorizes the sources of GHG emissions based not on the life cycle of the products but on sectors (e.g., energy, agriculture, etc), and each sector is broken down into activities or practices (e.g., mobile combustion, manure management, etc.). And for the GHG emissions related to manure management, there is the category of manure manage- ment, which covers CH4 and N2O emissions from livestock manure management; and thus a comparative LCA of manure management enables quantification of GHG mitigation in this category in line with the structure of the national GHG inventory, taking into account negative effects. This is the significance of conducting the comparative LCA of manure management in this study as well as the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming. The GHG mitigation potential brought about by the low- protein diet technique was estimated to be 340 Gg year using the GHG mitigation per head obtained in this study (19.9kg COe) and the annual number of mar- keted pigs (17.0 million heads; MAFF 2010a).

The results of cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming showed that LOW had lower GHG emissions than CNV throughout its life cycle (Fig. 4). The manure manage- ment process had the second highest GHG emissions (35% of the total) next to the feed production process in the pig life cycle, and thus the low-protein diet technique which mainly reduces GHGs emitted from the manure management process appears to be effective to reduce cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions from pig farming. The eutrophication potential was remarkably smaller in LOW without maximum GHG reduction than in CNV, and this difference appeared to be because more than half of the environmental loads related to eutrophication were generated in the manure management process in CNV, and effluent N, which is a major source of eutrophication, was reduced by the low-protein diet technique. Only a relatively small reduction in eutrophi- cation, however, was observed for LOW with maximum GHG reduction, and thus there are some tradeoffs between global warming and eutrophication in the present situation. As the reason for the greater energy consumption in LOW, it was suggested that the low- protein diet technique mainly reduced N2O emissions not

relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufactur- ing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. The difference in energy consumption between LOW and CNV was, however, even smaller (1%) in the case of effluent with a target N content of 100 mg L-1. The present LIME2 results suggest that LOW has a smaller overall environmental impact, and this appeared to be because LOW had smaller environmental impacts of global warming, acidification, and eutrophication than CNV while LOW consumed only a slightly larger amount of energy. The sensitivity analysis confirmed that LOW had smaller GHG emissions than CNV even in the least preferable case among the variations of the param- eters investigated in this analysis. The analysis also showed the possibility that the low-protein diet technique can bring about further reduction of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in pig farming.

In the present study, the productivity of pigs was defined as the same between LOW and CNV based on the reports mentioned above. Matsumoto et al. (2009) reported that the amino acid digestibilities of feeds for fattening pigs were reduced by 3-7% in a hot environ- ment (32°C), while Le Bellego et al. (2002) and Kerr et al. (2003) reported that no interaction between the low- protein diet supplemented with AA and high ambient temperature was observed with regard to weight gain of the pigs. Thus, increased AA supplementation might be needed during the hot season to prevent possible reduction of the productivity of pigs, although the effects of a hot environment on the amino acid requirements and weight gain of pigs should be further investigated.

Mosnier et al. (2011) conducted LCA of feed produc- tion with or without AA for use in France and reported that it was not clear whether low-protein pig feeds with AA showed environmental superiority in terms of GHG emissions, although it was clear in terms of some other environmental impact categories. The results of Lammers et al. (2010) also showed that the energy-related GHG emissions from and the energy consumption of feed production were increased by supplementation with AA, although they could be decreased by use of AA together with phytase. The results of the present study, however, suggest that the low-protein diet technique reduces the cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions and the integrated environmental impacts of pig production. Ogino et al. (2007) have reported that liquid feed production from food residues could remarkably reduce environmental impacts such as GHG emissions compared to the disposal of food residues by incineration combined with the import of commercial pig feeds; however, this measure depends on the local availability of food residues appropriate for feed use, and they are not sufficient to replace all the currently consumed pig feed. Nguyen et al. (2010) and Hishinuma et al. (2007)

suggested that biogas production (anaerobic digestion) from pig manure could reduce GHG emissions; however, the application of effluent after anaerobic digestion is very limited among "landless" pig producers, which are typical in countries such as Japan. In contrast, the low- protein diet technique does not depend on the availability of feedstock or the modification of waste treatment systems, and thus, theoretically, it can be used in all pig farms.

The GHG reduction effect of the low-protein diet technique, which reduces N excretion, is large in waste- water treatment having a high N2O emission factor, and the wastewater treatment of swine liquid manure is widely used in countries and regions that have a high density of animals, such as Japan, Korea (Shin et al. 2005), and Taiwan (Su et al. 1997). Furthermore, it has been pointed out that wastewater treatment of livestock effluent would increase to alleviate the increasing odor and over-application of nutrients as a result of the progressive intensification of livestock production in countries and regions where it has been managed in a slurry system or anaerobic lagoon system (Bernet and Béline 2009; Bortone 2009; Vanotti et al. 2009). The low-protein diet technique would then exert a GHG reduction effect over wider areas.

While the GHG emissions from AA manufacturing were predictably higher than those from the production of the main feed ingredients such as corn and soybean meal, the reduction of N2O emissions from manure management, mainly from wastewater treat- ment, was much larger than the GHG increase due to the use of AA. In the case in which the target N content in

the effluent from wastewater treatment was 100 mg L-¹ the GHG reduction in manure management brought about by the low-protein diet technique was even larger (Fig. 3). This appeared to be because a larger amount of the C source for denitrification (methanol) was needed for CNV in this case to decrease the N content in the effluent SO as to meet the stricter effluent standard, while LOW had much lower urinary N excretion and lower subsequent N content in liquid manure, leading to the use of only a small amount of methanol. The national GHG inventory, which provides a basis for policy- making related to GHG mitigation, categorizes the sources of GHG emissions based not on the life cycle of the products but on sectors (e.g., energy, agriculture, etc), and each sector is broken down into activities or practices (e.g., mobile combustion, manure management, etc.). And for the GHG emissions related to manure management, there is the category of manure manage- ment, which covers CH4 and N2O emissions from livestock manure management; and thus a comparative LCA of manure management enables quantification of GHG mitigation in this category in line with the structure of the national GHG inventory, taking into account negative effects. This is the significance of conducting the comparative LCA of manure management in this study as well as the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming. The GHG mitigation potential brought about by the low- protein diet technique was estimated to be 340 Gg year using the GHG mitigation per head obtained in this study (19.9kg COe) and the annual number of mar- keted pigs (17.0 million heads; MAFF 2010a).

The results of cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming showed that LOW had lower GHG emissions than CNV throughout its life cycle (Fig. 4). The manure manage- ment process had the second highest GHG emissions (35% of the total) next to the feed production process in the pig life cycle, and thus the low-protein diet technique which mainly reduces GHGs emitted from the manure management process appears to be effective to reduce cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions from pig farming. The eutrophication potential was remarkably smaller in LOW without maximum GHG reduction than in CNV, and this difference appeared to be because more than half of the environmental loads related to eutrophication were generated in the manure management process in CNV, and effluent N, which is a major source of eutrophication, was reduced by the low-protein diet technique. Only a relatively small reduction in eutrophi- cation, however, was observed for LOW with maximum GHG reduction, and thus there are some tradeoffs between global warming and eutrophication in the present situation. As the reason for the greater energy consumption in LOW, it was suggested that the low- protein diet technique mainly reduced N2O emissions not

relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufactur- ing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. The difference in energy consumption between LOW and CNV was, however, even smaller (1%) in the case of effluent with a target N content of 100 mg L-1. The present LIME2 results suggest that LOW has a smaller overall environmental impact, and this appeared to be because LOW had smaller environmental impacts of global warming, acidification, and eutrophication than CNV while LOW consumed only a slightly larger amount of energy. The sensitivity analysis confirmed that LOW had smaller GHG emissions than CNV even in the least preferable case among the variations of the param- eters investigated in this analysis. The analysis also showed the possibility that the low-protein diet technique can bring about further reduction of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in pig farming.

In the present study, the productivity of pigs was defined as the same between LOW and CNV based on the reports mentioned above. Matsumoto et al. (2009) reported that the amino acid digestibilities of feeds for fattening pigs were reduced by 3-7% in a hot environ- ment (32°C), while Le Bellego et al. (2002) and Kerr et al. (2003) reported that no interaction between the low- protein diet supplemented with AA and high ambient temperature was observed with regard to weight gain of the pigs. Thus, increased AA supplementation might be needed during the hot season to prevent possible reduction of the productivity of pigs, although the effects of a hot environment on the amino acid requirements and weight gain of pigs should be further investigated.

Mosnier et al. (2011) conducted LCA of feed produc- tion with or without AA for use in France and reported that it was not clear whether low-protein pig feeds with AA showed environmental superiority in terms of GHG emissions, although it was clear in terms of some other environmental impact categories. The results of Lammers et al. (2010) also showed that the energy-related GHG emissions from and the energy consumption of feed production were increased by supplementation with AA, although they could be decreased by use of AA together with phytase. The results of the present study, however, suggest that the low-protein diet technique reduces the cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions and the integrated environmental impacts of pig production. Ogino et al. (2007) have reported that liquid feed production from food residues could remarkably reduce environmental impacts such as GHG emissions compared to the disposal of food residues by incineration combined with the import of commercial pig feeds; however, this measure depends on the local availability of food residues appropriate for feed use, and they are not sufficient to replace all the currently consumed pig feed. Nguyen et al. (2010) and Hishinuma et al. (2007)

suggested that biogas production (anaerobic digestion) from pig manure could reduce GHG emissions; however, the application of effluent after anaerobic digestion is very limited among "landless" pig producers, which are typical in countries such as Japan. In contrast, the low- protein diet technique does not depend on the availability of feedstock or the modification of waste treatment systems, and thus, theoretically, it can be used in all pig farms.

The GHG reduction effect of the low-protein diet technique, which reduces N excretion, is large in waste- water treatment having a high N2O emission factor, and the wastewater treatment of swine liquid manure is widely used in countries and regions that have a high density of animals, such as Japan, Korea (Shin et al. 2005), and Taiwan (Su et al. 1997). Furthermore, it has been pointed out that wastewater treatment of livestock effluent would increase to alleviate the increasing odor and over-application of nutrients as a result of the progressive intensification of livestock production in countries and regions where it has been managed in a slurry system or anaerobic lagoon system (Bernet and Béline 2009; Bortone 2009; Vanotti et al. 2009). The low-protein diet technique would then exert a GHG reduction effect over wider areas.

diet supplemented with AA resulted in 20% lower GHG emissions, even taking into account negative effects of the changes in diet. While the GHG emissions from AA manufacturing were predictably higher than those from the production of the main feed ingredients such as corn and soybean meal, the reduction of N2O emissions from manure management, mainly from wastewater treat- ment, was much larger than the GHG increase due to the use of AA. In the case in which the target N content in

the effluent from wastewater treatment was 100 mg L-¹ the GHG reduction in manure management brought about by the low-protein diet technique was even larger (Fig. 3). This appeared to be because a larger amount of the C source for denitrification (methanol) was needed for CNV in this case to decrease the N content in the effluent SO as to meet the stricter effluent standard, while LOW had much lower urinary N excretion and lower subsequent N content in liquid manure, leading to the use of only a small amount of methanol. The national GHG inventory, which provides a basis for policy- making related to GHG mitigation, categorizes the sources of GHG emissions based not on the life cycle of the products but on sectors (e.g., energy, agriculture, etc), and each sector is broken down into activities or practices (e.g., mobile combustion, manure management, etc.). And for the GHG emissions related to manure management, there is the category of manure manage- ment, which covers CH4 and N2O emissions from livestock manure management; and thus a comparative LCA of manure management enables quantification of GHG mitigation in this category in line with the structure of the national GHG inventory, taking into account negative effects. This is the significance of conducting the comparative LCA of manure management in this study as well as the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming. The GHG mitigation potential brought about by the low- protein diet technique was estimated to be 340 Gg year using the GHG mitigation per head obtained in this study (19.9kg COe) and the annual number of mar- keted pigs (17.0 million heads; MAFF 2010a).

The results of cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming showed that LOW had lower GHG emissions than CNV throughout its life cycle (Fig. 4). The manure manage- ment process had the second highest GHG emissions (35% of the total) next to the feed production process in the pig life cycle, and thus the low-protein diet technique which mainly reduces GHGs emitted from the manure management process appears to be effective to reduce cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions from pig farming. The eutrophication potential was remarkably smaller in LOW without maximum GHG reduction than in CNV, and this difference appeared to be because more than half of the environmental loads related to eutrophication were generated in the manure management process in CNV, and effluent N, which is a major source of eutrophication, was reduced by the low-protein diet technique. Only a relatively small reduction in eutrophi- cation, however, was observed for LOW with maximum GHG reduction, and thus there are some tradeoffs between global warming and eutrophication in the present situation. As the reason for the greater energy consumption in LOW, it was suggested that the low- protein diet technique mainly reduced N2O emissions not

relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufactur- ing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. The difference in energy consumption between LOW and CNV was, however, even smaller (1%) in the case of effluent with a target N content of 100 mg L-1. The present LIME2 results suggest that LOW has a smaller overall environmental impact, and this appeared to be because LOW had smaller environmental impacts of global warming, acidification, and eutrophication than CNV while LOW consumed only a slightly larger amount of energy. The sensitivity analysis confirmed that LOW had smaller GHG emissions than CNV even in the least preferable case among the variations of the param- eters investigated in this analysis. The analysis also showed the possibility that the low-protein diet technique can bring about further reduction of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in pig farming.

In the present study, the productivity of pigs was defined as the same between LOW and CNV based on the reports mentioned above. Matsumoto et al. (2009) reported that the amino acid digestibilities of feeds for fattening pigs were reduced by 3-7% in a hot environ- ment (32°C), while Le Bellego et al. (2002) and Kerr et al. (2003) reported that no interaction between the low- protein diet supplemented with AA and high ambient temperature was observed with regard to weight gain of the pigs. Thus, increased AA supplementation might be needed during the hot season to prevent possible reduction of the productivity of pigs, although the effects of a hot environment on the amino acid requirements and weight gain of pigs should be further investigated.

Mosnier et al. (2011) conducted LCA of feed produc- tion with or without AA for use in France and reported that it was not clear whether low-protein pig feeds with AA showed environmental superiority in terms of GHG emissions, although it was clear in terms of some other environmental impact categories. The results of Lammers et al. (2010) also showed that the energy-related GHG emissions from and the energy consumption of feed production were increased by supplementation with AA, although they could be decreased by use of AA together with phytase. The results of the present study, however, suggest that the low-protein diet technique reduces the cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions and the integrated environmental impacts of pig production. Ogino et al. (2007) have reported that liquid feed production from food residues could remarkably reduce environmental impacts such as GHG emissions compared to the disposal of food residues by incineration combined with the import of commercial pig feeds; however, this measure depends on the local availability of food residues appropriate for feed use, and they are not sufficient to replace all the currently consumed pig feed. Nguyen et al. (2010) and Hishinuma et al. (2007)

suggested that biogas production (anaerobic digestion) from pig manure could reduce GHG emissions; however, the application of effluent after anaerobic digestion is very limited among "landless" pig producers, which are typical in countries such as Japan. In contrast, the low- protein diet technique does not depend on the availability of feedstock or the modification of waste treatment systems, and thus, theoretically, it can be used in all pig farms.

The GHG reduction effect of the low-protein diet technique, which reduces N excretion, is large in waste- water treatment having a high N2O emission factor, and the wastewater treatment of swine liquid manure is widely used in countries and regions that have a high density of animals, such as Japan, Korea (Shin et al. 2005), and Taiwan (Su et al. 1997). Furthermore, it has been pointed out that wastewater treatment of livestock effluent would increase to alleviate the increasing odor and over-application of nutrients as a result of the progressive intensification of livestock production in countries and regions where it has been managed in a slurry system or anaerobic lagoon system (Bernet and Béline 2009; Bortone 2009; Vanotti et al. 2009). The low-protein diet technique would then exert a GHG reduction effect over wider areas.

diet supplemented with AA resulted in 20% lower GHG emissions, even taking into account negative effects of the changes in diet. While the GHG emissions from AA manufacturing were predictably higher than those from the production of the main feed ingredients such as corn and soybean meal, the reduction of N2O emissions from manure management, mainly from wastewater treat- ment, was much larger than the GHG increase due to the use of AA. In the case in which the target N content in

the effluent from wastewater treatment was 100 mg L-¹ the GHG reduction in manure management brought about by the low-protein diet technique was even larger (Fig. 3). This appeared to be because a larger amount of the C source for denitrification (methanol) was needed for CNV in this case to decrease the N content in the effluent SO as to meet the stricter effluent standard, while LOW had much lower urinary N excretion and lower subsequent N content in liquid manure, leading to the use of only a small amount of methanol. The national GHG inventory, which provides a basis for policy- making related to GHG mitigation, categorizes the sources of GHG emissions based not on the life cycle of the products but on sectors (e.g., energy, agriculture, etc), and each sector is broken down into activities or practices (e.g., mobile combustion, manure management, etc.). And for the GHG emissions related to manure management, there is the category of manure manage- ment, which covers CH4 and N2O emissions from livestock manure management; and thus a comparative LCA of manure management enables quantification of GHG mitigation in this category in line with the structure of the national GHG inventory, taking into account negative effects. This is the significance of conducting the comparative LCA of manure management in this study as well as the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming. The GHG mitigation potential brought about by the low- protein diet technique was estimated to be 340 Gg year using the GHG mitigation per head obtained in this study (19.9kg COe) and the annual number of mar- keted pigs (17.0 million heads; MAFF 2010a).

The results of cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming showed that LOW had lower GHG emissions than CNV throughout its life cycle (Fig. 4). The manure manage- ment process had the second highest GHG emissions (35% of the total) next to the feed production process in the pig life cycle, and thus the low-protein diet technique which mainly reduces GHGs emitted from the manure management process appears to be effective to reduce cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions from pig farming. The eutrophication potential was remarkably smaller in LOW without maximum GHG reduction than in CNV, and this difference appeared to be because more than half of the environmental loads related to eutrophication were generated in the manure management process in CNV, and effluent N, which is a major source of eutrophication, was reduced by the low-protein diet technique. Only a relatively small reduction in eutrophi- cation, however, was observed for LOW with maximum GHG reduction, and thus there are some tradeoffs between global warming and eutrophication in the present situation. As the reason for the greater energy consumption in LOW, it was suggested that the low- protein diet technique mainly reduced N2O emissions not

relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufactur- ing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. The difference in energy consumption between LOW and CNV was, however, even smaller (1%) in the case of effluent with a target N content of 100 mg L-1. The present LIME2 results suggest that LOW has a smaller overall environmental impact, and this appeared to be because LOW had smaller environmental impacts of global warming, acidification, and eutrophication than CNV while LOW consumed only a slightly larger amount of energy. The sensitivity analysis confirmed that LOW had smaller GHG emissions than CNV even in the least preferable case among the variations of the param- eters investigated in this analysis. The analysis also showed the possibility that the low-protein diet technique can bring about further reduction of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in pig farming.

In the present study, the productivity of pigs was defined as the same between LOW and CNV based on the reports mentioned above. Matsumoto et al. (2009) reported that the amino acid digestibilities of feeds for fattening pigs were reduced by 3-7% in a hot environ- ment (32°C), while Le Bellego et al. (2002) and Kerr et al. (2003) reported that no interaction between the low- protein diet supplemented with AA and high ambient temperature was observed with regard to weight gain of the pigs. Thus, increased AA supplementation might be needed during the hot season to prevent possible reduction of the productivity of pigs, although the effects of a hot environment on the amino acid requirements and weight gain of pigs should be further investigated.

Mosnier et al. (2011) conducted LCA of feed produc- tion with or without AA for use in France and reported that it was not clear whether low-protein pig feeds with AA showed environmental superiority in terms of GHG emissions, although it was clear in terms of some other environmental impact categories. The results of Lammers et al. (2010) also showed that the energy-related GHG emissions from and the energy consumption of feed production were increased by supplementation with AA, although they could be decreased by use of AA together with phytase. The results of the present study, however, suggest that the low-protein diet technique reduces the cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions and the integrated environmental impacts of pig production. Ogino et al. (2007) have reported that liquid feed production from food residues could remarkably reduce environmental impacts such as GHG emissions compared to the disposal of food residues by incineration combined with the import of commercial pig feeds; however, this measure depends on the local availability of food residues appropriate for feed use, and they are not sufficient to replace all the currently consumed pig feed. Nguyen et al. (2010) and Hishinuma et al. (2007)

suggested that biogas production (anaerobic digestion) from pig manure could reduce GHG emissions; however, the application of effluent after anaerobic digestion is very limited among "landless" pig producers, which are typical in countries such as Japan. In contrast, the low- protein diet technique does not depend on the availability of feedstock or the modification of waste treatment systems, and thus, theoretically, it can be used in all pig farms.

The GHG reduction effect of the low-protein diet technique, which reduces N excretion, is large in waste- water treatment having a high N2O emission factor, and the wastewater treatment of swine liquid manure is widely used in countries and regions that have a high density of animals, such as Japan, Korea (Shin et al. 2005), and Taiwan (Su et al. 1997). Furthermore, it has been pointed out that wastewater treatment of livestock effluent would increase to alleviate the increasing odor and over-application of nutrients as a result of the progressive intensification of livestock production in countries and regions where it has been managed in a slurry system or anaerobic lagoon system (Bernet and Béline 2009; Bortone 2009; Vanotti et al. 2009). The low-protein diet technique would then exert a GHG reduction effect over wider areas.

diet supplemented with AA resulted in 20% lower GHG emissions, even taking into account negative effects of the changes in diet. While the GHG emissions from AA manufacturing were predictably higher than those from the production of the main feed ingredients such as corn and soybean meal, the reduction of N2O emissions from manure management, mainly from wastewater treat- ment, was much larger than the GHG increase due to the use of AA. In the case in which the target N content in

the effluent from wastewater treatment was 100 mg L-¹ the GHG reduction in manure management brought about by the low-protein diet technique was even larger (Fig. 3). This appeared to be because a larger amount of the C source for denitrification (methanol) was needed for CNV in this case to decrease the N content in the effluent SO as to meet the stricter effluent standard, while LOW had much lower urinary N excretion and lower subsequent N content in liquid manure, leading to the use of only a small amount of methanol. The national GHG inventory, which provides a basis for policy- making related to GHG mitigation, categorizes the sources of GHG emissions based not on the life cycle of the products but on sectors (e.g., energy, agriculture, etc), and each sector is broken down into activities or practices (e.g., mobile combustion, manure management, etc.). And for the GHG emissions related to manure management, there is the category of manure manage- ment, which covers CH4 and N2O emissions from livestock manure management; and thus a comparative LCA of manure management enables quantification of GHG mitigation in this category in line with the structure of the national GHG inventory, taking into account negative effects. This is the significance of conducting the comparative LCA of manure management in this study as well as the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming. The GHG mitigation potential brought about by the low- protein diet technique was estimated to be 340 Gg year using the GHG mitigation per head obtained in this study (19.9kg COe) and the annual number of mar- keted pigs (17.0 million heads; MAFF 2010a).

The results of cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming showed that LOW had lower GHG emissions than CNV throughout its life cycle (Fig. 4). The manure manage- ment process had the second highest GHG emissions (35% of the total) next to the feed production process in the pig life cycle, and thus the low-protein diet technique which mainly reduces GHGs emitted from the manure management process appears to be effective to reduce cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions from pig farming. The eutrophication potential was remarkably smaller in LOW without maximum GHG reduction than in CNV, and this difference appeared to be because more than half of the environmental loads related to eutrophication were generated in the manure management process in CNV, and effluent N, which is a major source of eutrophication, was reduced by the low-protein diet technique. Only a relatively small reduction in eutrophi- cation, however, was observed for LOW with maximum GHG reduction, and thus there are some tradeoffs between global warming and eutrophication in the present situation. As the reason for the greater energy consumption in LOW, it was suggested that the low- protein diet technique mainly reduced N2O emissions not

relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufactur- ing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. The difference in energy consumption between LOW and CNV was, however, even smaller (1%) in the case of effluent with a target N content of 100 mg L-1. The present LIME2 results suggest that LOW has a smaller overall environmental impact, and this appeared to be because LOW had smaller environmental impacts of global warming, acidification, and eutrophication than CNV while LOW consumed only a slightly larger amount of energy. The sensitivity analysis confirmed that LOW had smaller GHG emissions than CNV even in the least preferable case among the variations of the param- eters investigated in this analysis. The analysis also showed the possibility that the low-protein diet technique can bring about further reduction of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in pig farming.

In the present study, the productivity of pigs was defined as the same between LOW and CNV based on the reports mentioned above. Matsumoto et al. (2009) reported that the amino acid digestibilities of feeds for fattening pigs were reduced by 3-7% in a hot environ- ment (32°C), while Le Bellego et al. (2002) and Kerr et al. (2003) reported that no interaction between the low- protein diet supplemented with AA and high ambient temperature was observed with regard to weight gain of the pigs. Thus, increased AA supplementation might be needed during the hot season to prevent possible reduction of the productivity of pigs, although the effects of a hot environment on the amino acid requirements and weight gain of pigs should be further investigated.

(2011) conducted LCA of feed produc- tion with or without AA for use in France and reported that it was not clear whether low-protein pig feeds with AA showed environmental superiority in terms of GHG emissions, although it was clear in terms of some other environmental impact categories. As the reason for the greater energy consumption in LOW, it was suggested that the low- protein diet technique mainly reduced N2O emissions not

relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufactur- ing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. The difference in energy consumption between LOW and CNV was, however, even smaller (1%) in the case of effluent with a target N content of 100 mg L-1. The present LIME2 results suggest that LOW has a smaller overall environmental impact, and this appeared to be because LOW had smaller environmental impacts of global warming, acidification, and eutrophication than CNV while LOW consumed only a slightly larger amount of energy. The sensitivity analysis confirmed that LOW had smaller GHG emissions than CNV even in the least preferable case among the variations of the param- eters investigated in this analysis. The analysis also showed the possibility that the low-protein diet technique can bring about further reduction of GHG emissions resulting from manure management in pig farming.

In the present study, the productivity of pigs was defined as the same between LOW and CNV based on the reports mentioned above. Matsumoto et al. (2009) reported that the amino acid digestibilities of feeds for fattening pigs were reduced by 3-7% in a hot environ- ment (32°C), while Le Bellego et al. (2002) and Kerr et al. (2003) reported that no interaction between the low- protein diet supplemented with AA and high ambient temperature was observed with regard to weight gain of the pigs. Thus, increased AA supplementation might be needed during the hot season to prevent possible reduction of the productivity of pigs, although the effects of a hot environment on the amino acid requirements and weight gain of pigs should be further investigated.

Mosnier et al. (2011) conducted LCA of feed produc- tion with or without AA for use in France and reported that it was not clear whether low-protein pig feeds with AA showed environmental superiority in terms of GHG emissions, although it was clear in terms of some other environmental impact categories. The results of Lammers et al. (2010) also showed that the energy-related GHG emissions from and the energy consumption of feed production were increased by supplementation with AA, although they could be decreased by use of AA together with phytase. The results of the present study, however, suggest that the low-protein diet technique reduces the cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions and the integrated environmental impacts of pig production. Ogino et al. (2007) have reported that liquid feed production from food residues could remarkably reduce environmental impacts such as GHG emissions compared to the disposal of food residues by incineration combined with the import of commercial pig feeds; however, this measure depends on the local availability of food residues appropriate for feed use, and they are not sufficient to replace all the currently consumed pig feed. Nguyen et al. (2010) and Hishinuma et al. (2007)

suggested that biogas production (anaerobic digestion) from pig manure could reduce GHG emissions; however, the application of effluent after anaerobic digestion is very limited among "landless" pig producers, which are typical in countries such as Japan. In contrast, the low- protein diet technique does not depend on the availability of feedstock or the modification of waste treatment systems, and thus, theoretically, it can be used in all pig farms.

The GHG reduction effect of the low-protein diet technique, which reduces N excretion, is large in waste- water treatment having a high N2O emission factor, and the wastewater treatment of swine liquid manure is widely used in countries and regions that have a high density of animals, such as Japan, Korea (Shin et al. 2005), and Taiwan (Su et al. 1997). Furthermore, it has been pointed out that wastewater treatment of livestock effluent would increase to alleviate the increasing odor and over-application of nutrients as a result of the progressive intensification of livestock production in countries and regions where it has been managed in a slurry system or anaerobic lagoon system (Bernet and Béline 2009; Bortone 2009; Vanotti et al. 2009). The low-protein diet technique would then exert a GHG reduction effect over wider areas.

The national GHG inventory, which provides a basis for policy- making related to GHG mitigation, categorizes the sources of GHG emissions based not on the life cycle of the products but on sectors (e.g., energy, agriculture, etc), and each sector is broken down into activities or practices (e.g., mobile combustion, manure management, etc.). And for the GHG emissions related to manure management, there is the category of manure manage- ment, which covers CH4 and N2O emissions from livestock manure management; and thus a comparative LCA of manure management enables quantification of GHG mitigation in this category in line with the structure of the national GHG inventory, taking into account negative effects. This is the significance of conducting the comparative LCA of manure management in this study as well as the cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming. The GHG mitigation potential brought about by the low- protein diet technique was estimated to be 340 Gg year using the GHG mitigation per head obtained in this study (19.9kg COe) and the annual number of mar- keted pigs (17.0 million heads; MAFF 2010a).

The results of cradle-to-farm gate LCA of pig farming showed that LOW had lower GHG emissions than CNV throughout its life cycle (Fig. 4). 42: The cradle-to-farm gate GHG emissions, acidification potential, eutrophication potential, and energy consump- tion per kg liveweight of CNV and LOW in the present state were calculated from the results obtained in this study to be 3.16 and 2.99 kg CO2e, 21.7 and 20.7 SOe, 9.1 and 6.6g PO4e, and 25.5 and respectively. Several research results of the LCA of pig farming have been reported, and cradle-to-farm gate environmental impacts per kg liveweight range from 2.3 to 4.0 kg CO2e for global warming (Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005; Liang et al. 2007; Nguyen et al. 2010; Pelletier et al. 2010), from 22.6 to 43.5 SO2e for acidification (Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005), from 15.9 to 45.9 PO4e for eutrophication (Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005; Pelletier et al. 2010), and from 6.8 to 22.21 for energy consumption (Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005; Nguyen et al. 2010; Pelletier et al. 2010), depending on the farming system, productivity, and energy recovery from manure. Different assumptions and emissions factors were also applied in these different studies, and thus precise comparison is difficult; how- ever, the present results are fairly consistent with the previously reported values. Some LCA studies on pig farming have also been reported. For example, Eriksson et al. (2005) compared pig farming systems using imported soybean meal as a protein source or locally
grown peas (Pisum sativum L.) and rapeseed meal (Brassica napus L.); however, they evaluated only the fattening stage of the pigs, and thus their results cannot be directly compared to ours.

Our results suggest that the use of the low-protein diet supplemented with AA in pig farming has lower GHG emissions, acidification potential, eutrophication poten- tial, and overall environmental impact than the conven- tional system. The use of the low-protein diet in pig farming therefore should be considered, given its envi- ronmental performance.
